name: Machine Learning Fundamentals III
description: Master advanced model evaluation, hyperparameter optimization, and complex data preprocessing pitfalls.

questions.question: Which evaluation metric is most appropriate for a highly imbalanced classification task where false positives are extremely costly?
options: ROC-AUC
Precision
Recall
Accuracy
questions.explain: Precision focuses on minimizing false positives by measuring the ratio of true positives to all predicted positives.

questions.question: What is a major limitation of using standard Grid Search for hyperparameter tuning on complex models?
options: It always fails to find the global optimum parameters.
It scales poorly as the number of hyperparameters increases.
It introduces target data leakage during the search phase.
It restricts the model to evaluating only linear parameters.
questions.explain: Grid Search evaluates every possible combination of parameters, making it computationally expensive and slow for large search spaces.

questions.question: How does Bayesian Optimization improve upon Random Search for hyperparameter tuning?
options: It runs all parameter evaluations in parallel pipelines.
It uses past evaluation results to choose the next parameters.
It eliminates the need to use a validation dataset entirely.
It simplifies the model architecture to speed up execution.
questions.explain: Bayesian Optimization builds a probabilistic model of the objective function to select the most promising hyperparameters sequentially.

questions.question: What type of data leakage occurs when a time-series model uses future information to predict past events?
options: Target leakage
Temporal leakage
Feature scaling leakage
Distributional leakage
questions.explain: Temporal leakage happens when data from the future inadvertently leaks into the training data of a time-dependent model.

questions.question: Which cross-validation strategy should be strictly used when evaluating time-series forecasting models?
options: Stratified K-Fold Cross-Validation
Time Series Split (Rolling Window)
Repeated Random Subsampling
Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
questions.explain: Time series splits respect the temporal order of data, ensuring the model is never trained on future data to predict the past.

questions.question: What is the primary effect of adding an L1 regularization term (Lasso) to a linear modeling process?
options: It forces the model weights to scale exponentially.
It drives coefficients to zero, performing feature selection.
It eliminates the need to normalize numerical features.
It increases the model sensitivity to high-frequency noise.
questions.explain: L1 regularization penalizes the absolute magnitude of coefficients, which can shrink unimportant feature weights exactly to zero.

questions.question: In the context of tree-based models, what does the 'feature importance' metric typically measure?
options: The exact linear correlation with the target.
The total reduction in criterion brought by a feature.
The storage size required to compress the feature.
The percentage of missing values within that feature.
questions.explain: Feature importance in tree models calculates how much each feature decreases the impurity or splits the nodes effectively across all trees.

questions.question: Why is it dangerous to compute the mean and variance on the entire dataset before splitting into train/test sets?
options: It causes an immediate categorical encoding error.
It leaks test set statistics into the training process.
It artificially reduces the dimensionality of features.
It changes the target labels from discrete to continuous.
questions.explain: Calculating preprocessing statistics on the whole dataset introduces information from the test set into training, causing data leakage.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of using Stratified K-Fold over standard K-Fold cross-validation?
options: It completes the training phase in half the time.
It preserves the percentage of samples for each class.
It generates synthetic rows to balance out the data.
It automatically optimizes the model learning rate.
questions.explain: Stratification ensures that each fold contains approximately the same percentage of target class labels as the complete dataset.

questions.question: What does the 'early stopping' technique monitor to prevent overfitting during XGBoost training?
options: The training dataset loss trend.
The validation dataset metric trend.
The total number of features used.
The execution time per iteration.
questions.explain: Early stopping halts training when the performance on a separate validation dataset stops improving after a set number of rounds.
